comments:
Summary:
This paper proposes a neural graph-based dependency parser that replaces the common concatenation-based attention with a deep biaffine attention mechanism (bilinear + bias) and uses MLPs to reduce dimensionality of BiLSTM outputs before scoring arcs and labels. The authors also present an extensive set of practical hyperparameter decisions (depth, recurrent cell variants, optimizer settings, tag dropout, etc.) and show that the proposed model achieves state-of-the-art UAS/LAS on standard English and competitive results on Chinese.

Strengths:
- The modeling idea (deep biaffine scoring for arcs and labels) is simple, well-motivated, and computationally efficient compared to a concatenation-based attention that explicitly constructs pairwise tensors.
- The paper contains thorough empirical investigation of hyperparameters that materially affect performance (LSTM depth, MLPs, Adam β2, tag dropout, recurrent dropout), which is valuable for practitioners.
- Comparisons are made against several strong prior systems and the model shows clear gains on standard PTB benchmarks.
- The approach to label prediction (biaffine mapping to a vector of class scores) is a clean, parallel extension of the arc scoring method.

Weaknesses / Issues to address:
- While the paper explores many hyperparameters, some ablations could be more detailed. In particular: (a) the impact of tag dropout on Chinese LAS (where the model underperforms) is hypothesized but not quantified with controlled experiments; (b) the role of pretrained embeddings in label prediction (again for CTB) is only speculated about. Explicit ablations would strengthen those claims.
- All reported experiments appear to use gold POS tags for Chinese and predicted tags for English; the paper should explicitly report Chinese results with predicted tags (or at least discuss expectations) to help assess real-world performance.
- Although runtime/memory advantages are discussed qualitatively and some parsing speeds are given, more complete runtime and memory comparisons across configurations (concatenation vs biaffine; 2 vs 4 layers) would help readers understand trade-offs.
- The provided excerpt contains some repetitions and formatting glitches (likely artifact of the extraction); the authors should ensure a clean final manuscript. This does not appear to affect the technical content.

Suggestions:
- Add targeted ablations quantifying the effect of tag dropout and pretrained embedding quality on LAS for CTB.
- Report results on predicted POS tags for CTB to mirror the English setting, or discuss how POS-tagging differences affect comparisons.
- If not already planned, release code and hyperparameter settings to improve reproducibility, since many of the gains come from careful tuning.
- Consider reporting statistical significance for the main gains over previous state-of-the-art.

Overall assessment:
This is a technically sound and practically useful contribution to neural dependency parsing. The modeling change is conceptually straightforward yet effective, and the exploration of optimization and regularization choices is valuable. The paper would be of high interest to the NLP community and appropriate for an oral presentation slot at a major conference.
